Caption: Axial pressure computed from the O’Neil integral for normal velocity ranging from 1 to 100 mm/s by step of 10 mm/s. The pressure is normalized to atmospheric pressure p atm = 10 5 Pa. The colormap goes from yellow to dark purple for increasing velocities. The inset plots the corresponding dimensionless axial pressure profiles as defined by Eq. (1).
Validation of the numerical simulations using k-wave (circle markers) against the O’Neil integral solution (solid line).

Time sequence of ultrasound-driven bubble clouds showing 6 frames out of 68, with a time interval of 333 μs. The sampling frequency is 30 kHz. The framed image on the right-hand side corresponds to the minimum intensity projection (MinIP) of the 68 frames. The full movie can be seen in the supplementary material (Video 1).
Time sequence of cavitation bubble cloud in a 250-kHz HIFU field. The first frame corresponds t = 0 . The interframe time is 40 μs.
Acoustic simulation of the pressure field in the presence of a spherical bubble cloud, represented by a single bubble of radius 0.5 mm.

Bubble size distribution, at their maximum size, typically observed for a bubble cloud nucleating in the focal region with a frame rate of 100 kHz. The error on the maximum bubble size is estimated to be 8%. (Top) First frames where bubbles are observed. 75% of the detected volumes have been identified as individual bubbles and used to plot the distribution (22 bubbles). (Bottom) Bubble size distribution 1.5 acoustic periods later. 60% of the detected volumes have been identified as individual bubbles and used to plot the distribution (53 bubbles).

Minimum intensity projection from a single experiment showing hundreds of ultrasound-driven bubble clouds in a stone-free environment. The camera sampling frequency is 50 kHz. The 250-kHz-transducer operated in burst mode at 10% duty cycle (500 cycles, 2 ms). The present projection has been compiled from the first 18 ms. The dashed red lines contours the pressure region where p ∗ > p c ∗ as extracted from the numerical simulation.

[(a-b), left panels] Interaction of bubbles clouds with an hemispherical stone of 20 mm in diameter 20 μs after the ultrasound burst starts. [(a-b), right panels] Falling fragments of stone captured 4.8 ms and 18.8 ms after the end of the ultrasonic excitation. Each image width is 4 mm. A movie showing falling fragments is shown in supplementary material (Video 2).

Measurement of the normalized pixel intensity I ∗ over the polar coordinate θ given in radian. The solid dark line and the red dashed lines have been extracted from the top left and right images, respectively. The left image is 60 μs after the ultrasound burst starts, and the right image is 23.6 ms later. Intensity fluctuations result from bubbles or stone fragments along the optical path. Each image width is 4 mm.

Comparison of the pressure field computed from the acoustic simulations against the MinIP-compiled shadowgraph of the corresponding experiment run over one burst. The stone is a 20-mm diameter hemisphere. Regions Π 1 and Π 2 locate the high-pressure regions where the pressure cavitation threshold is reached, p ∗ > p c ∗ . A video showing cavitation activity in both regions is provided in supplementary material (Video 3).

Trajectory of bubble clouds along the x and z-direction as time proceeds. The physical time is normalized by the acoustic period τ a . The two left graphs correspond to experiments with a stone, and the two right plots correspond to bubble clouds propagating in a stone-free environment. The B i with i = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 refer to the initial subclouds. The cloud denoted B 123 is formed from the merging of B 1 , B 2 and B 3 .

Comparison of the pressure field computed from the acoustic simulations against the MinIP-compiled shadowgraph of the corresponding experiment run over one ultrasound burst. The stone has a flat surface of 35-mm side length. Again, regions Π 1 and Π 2 locate the high-pressure regions where p ∗ > p c ∗ .

Axial pressure normalized to the cavitation pressure threshold, and surface damage coefficients over the z-direction. Surface damage coefficients are defined with Table 2. The thick solid line corresponds to the O’Neil integral solutions, and the thick dashed line is the theoretical reflected pressure at stone surface. The circle markers shows the simulated reflected pressure. The dotted-dashed line is the geometrical focal distance. The dotted line plots the cavitation pressure threshold.

Tomography of the surface damages at the proximal pole of a 20-mm diameter hemispherical model stone after being exposed to focused ultrasound. The 250-kHz-transducer was operated in burst mode at 10% duty cycle (500 cycles, 2 ms) during 2 s. The tomography has been performed at the beamline ID19 of the European Synchrotron Research Facility. Additional details on the optical alignment of the tomograph are given in Appendix B.
